title: Probability & Counting
blurb: Master probability rules, the 'at least one' shortcut, and counting with permutations and combinations.
theory:
What Is Probability?

Probability measures how likely an event is. For equally likely outcomes, P(event) = (number of favorable outcomes) ÷ (total number of possible outcomes). Every probability is a number from 0 to 1: P = 0 means impossible, P = 1 means certain. You can write it as a fraction, decimal, or percent.

Example: a fair die has 6 faces. P(rolling a 4) = 1/6. P(rolling an even number) = 3/6 = 1/2.

The Complement Rule

The complement of event A is 'A does not happen.' Since something either happens or it doesn't, P(not A) = 1 − P(A). This is one of the most powerful tools on the GRE because it is often far easier to count what you do NOT want.

Combining Two Events: OR (Addition)

For 'A or B,' use the addition rule. If events overlap (can both happen): P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) − P(A and B). You subtract the overlap so it is not counted twice.

If events are mutually exclusive (cannot both happen, like rolling a 2 OR a 5 on one die), then P(A and B) = 0, so P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B).

Combining Two Events: AND (Multiplication)

For 'A and B,' multiply. If events are independent (one does not affect the other, like two coin flips): P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B).

If events are dependent (one affects the next, like drawing cards without replacement): P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B given A). The second probability uses updated totals.

SituationRule
A or B (overlapping)P(A) + P(B) − P(A and B)
A or B (mutually exclusive)P(A) + P(B)
A and B (independent)P(A) × P(B)
A and B (dependent)P(A) × P(B after A)

Without Replacement

When you draw items and do not put them back, both the favorable count and the total shrink. From a bag of 5 red and 3 blue (8 total), P(two reds in a row) = 5/8 × 4/7 = 20/56 = 5/14. Notice both numerator and denominator drop by 1.

'At Least One' — The Complement Trick

'At least one' is the GRE's favorite probability phrase. Computing it directly means adding up exactly one, exactly two, exactly three... Instead use: P(at least one) = 1 − P(none). Find the probability that the event happens zero times, then subtract from 1.

Example: flip a fair coin 3 times. P(at least one head) = 1 − P(no heads) = 1 − (1/2)3 = 1 − 1/8 = 7/8.

The Fundamental Counting Principle

If one choice can be made in m ways and a second independent choice in n ways, together they can be made in m × n ways. This extends to any number of stages: multiply the count at each stage. A 4-digit PIN with no repeated digits: 10 × 9 × 8 × 7 = 5040 ways.

Factorials

n! (n factorial) = n × (n−1) × (n−2) × ... × 2 × 1. It counts the number of ways to arrange n distinct items in a row. By definition 0! = 1. Examples: 5! = 120, 4! = 24, 3! = 6.

Permutations — Order Matters

A permutation counts arrangements where order matters (rankings, seatings, passwords). The number of ways to arrange r items chosen from n is nPr = n! ÷ (n − r)!. Example: choosing a president, then a VP from 7 people: 7P2 = 7!/5! = 7 × 6 = 42.

Combinations — Order Doesn't Matter

A combination counts selections where order does NOT matter (committees, teams, handshakes). nCr = n! ÷ [r! × (n − r)!]. Because order is irrelevant, you divide the permutation count by r! to remove duplicate orderings. Example: choosing a 2-person committee from 7: 7C2 = 21. Useful fact: nCr = nC(n−r), so 6C2 = 6C4.

Sets & Venn Diagrams

For two overlapping groups, the count in 'A or B' follows the same logic as probability: |A or B| = |A| + |B| − |A and B|. To find how many are in neither group: Neither = Total − (|A| + |B| − |Both|). A Venn diagram with two circles helps you place the 'both' region first, then fill the rest.
whatEtsTests:
• The 'at least one' complement shortcut — by far the most common probability twist on the GRE.
  • Distinguishing permutations (order matters) from combinations (order does not) in committee/arrangement word problems.
  • Dependent probability with 'without replacement' phrasing where the denominator changes each draw.
  • The addition rule with overlap subtraction, and the two-set/Venn 'neither' formula.
  • Multiplying stages with the fundamental counting principle, often with restrictions like 'no repeats.'
  • Quantitative comparison questions exploiting nCr = nC(n−r) symmetry.
tips: At least one = 1 − P(none). Always check whether finding the complement is easier than direct counting.
Order matters → permutation (nPr); order doesn't → combination (nCr). Committees and teams are combinations.
nCr always equals nC(n−r), so 10C7 = 10C3. Compute the smaller one.
For 'and' multiply, for 'or' add. Independent events multiply; mutually exclusive events add with no overlap.
Without replacement: drop both numerator and denominator by one each draw.
Two-set total: Neither = Total − (A + B − Both). Subtract the overlap once.
traps: Confusing permutations and combinations — using nPr for a committee (order doesn't matter) overcounts by r!.
Forgetting to subtract the overlap P(A and B) when events can both occur, inflating P(A or B).
Treating dependent draws as independent — keeping the denominator fixed in 'without replacement' problems.
Adding probabilities when you should multiply (or vice versa) by misreading 'and' vs 'or'.
Computing 'at least one' by direct case-counting and missing a case, instead of using 1 − P(none).

examples.q: A bag holds 5 red and 3 blue marbles. Two marbles are drawn one after another without replacement. What is the probability that both are red?
examples.steps:
1) First draw: 5 red of 8 total, so P(red) = 5/8.

2) After removing one red, 4 red of 7 remain, so P(red next) = 4/7.

3) Multiply (dependent 'and'): 5/8 × 4/7 = 20/56 = 5/14.

examples.q: A fair coin is flipped 3 times. What is the probability of getting at least one head?
examples.steps:
1) Use the complement: P(at least one head) = 1 − P(no heads).

2) P(no heads) = P(all tails) = (1/2)3 = 1/8.

3) So P(at least one head) = 1 − 1/8 = 7/8.
examples.shortcut: Counting directly means adding 3 cases; complement turns it into one quick calculation: 1 − 1/8 = 7/8.

examples.q: A committee of 2 people is chosen from a group of 7. How many different committees are possible?
examples.steps:
1) Order does not matter (a committee of {A,B} = {B,A}), so use combinations.

2) 7C2 = 7! ÷ (2! × 5!) = (7 × 6) ÷ (2 × 1).

3) = 42 ÷ 2 = 21 committees.

examples.q: In a class of 30 students, 18 take Algebra and 15 take Biology; 7 take both. How many take neither subject?
examples.steps:
1) Students taking at least one = 18 + 15 − 7 = 26 (subtract the overlap once).

2) Neither = Total − (at least one) = 30 − 26.

3) = 4 students take neither.

questions.q: A fair six-sided die is rolled once. What is the probability of rolling a number greater than 4?
questions.explain: Numbers greater than 4 are 5 and 6, so 2 favorable outcomes out of 6. P = 2/6 = 1/3.

questions.q: The probability that it rains tomorrow is 0.3. What is the probability that it does NOT rain tomorrow?
questions.explain: By the complement rule, P(not rain) = 1 − P(rain) = 1 − 0.3 = 0.7.

questions.q: How many distinct ways can 4 different books be arranged in a row on a shelf? Enter the number.
Two fair coins are flipped. What is the probability that both land on heads?
questions.explain: The flips are independent, so multiply: P(H and H) = 1/2 × 1/2 = 1/4.

questions.q: A jar contains 4 green, 3 yellow, and 5 red candies. One candy is drawn at random. What is the probability it is NOT red?
questions.explain: Total = 4 + 3 + 5 = 12. Not red = green + yellow = 7. P(not red) = 7/12. (Equivalently 1 − 5/12 = 7/12.)

questions.q: From a group of 5 men and 4 women, a committee of 3 is selected at random. What is the probability the committee includes at least one woman?
questions.explain: Use the complement. Total committees = 9C3 = 84. Committees with no women (all 3 men) = 5C3 = 10. P(at least one woman) = 1 − 10/84 = 74/84 = 37/42.

questions.q: A 3-digit code is formed using digits 0 through 9, where no digit may repeat. How many different codes are possible?
questions.explain: By the fundamental counting principle with no repeats: 10 × 9 × 8 = 720.

questions.qa: The number of ways to choose a committee of 2 from the group
questions.qb: The number of ways to choose a committee of 4 from the group
questions.explain: 6C2 = 15 and 6C4 = 15. By the symmetry nCr = nC(n−r), they are equal. The two quantities are equal.

questions.q: Events A and B can both occur. P(A) = 0.4, P(B) = 0.3, and P(A and B) = 0.1. What is P(A or B)? Enter a decimal.
questions.explain: Addition rule with overlap: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) − P(A and B) = 0.4 + 0.3 − 0.1 = 0.6.

questions.q: A standard 52-card deck has 4 aces. Two cards are drawn without replacement. What is the probability that both cards are aces?
Five distinct books, including a specific math book and a specific science book, are arranged in a row. What is the probability that the math and science books are next to each other?
questions.explain: Total arrangements = 5! = 120. Treat the two specific books as one block: 4! ways to arrange the blocks, times 2! for their internal order = 24 × 2 = 48 favorable. P = 48/120 = 2/5.

questions.q: A fair die is rolled twice. Which of the following statements are TRUE? Indicate all that apply.
questions.explain: P(no sixes) = (5/6)2 = 25/36, so P(at least one six) = 1 − 25/36 = 11/36: both true. Sum 7 occurs 6 of 36 ways = 1/6: true. Both rolls equal occurs 6 of 36 ways = 1/6: true. Sum 12 occurs only once (6,6) = 1/36, not 1/12: false. So the true statements are the first four.
